g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Congestion Control in Computer Networks, a critical concept for network stability.
Network congestion occurs when demand exceeds available resources, causing delays, packet loss, and reduced throughput.
g.text: A state where network resources cannot handle aggregate traffic demand.
Results in: Increased delays, Packet loss, Reduced throughput, Network instability

g.data-narration: Congestion arises from five main causes: insufficient bandwidth, router buffer overflow, slow processing, improper window sizes, and synchronized packet drops.
Flow control prevents sender from overwhelming the receiver (end-to-end), while congestion control prevents sender from overwhelming the network (entire path).
The Leaky Bucket algorithm shapes traffic by outputting at a constant rate regardless of input, converting bursty traffic into smooth flow.
g.text: Traffic shaping mechanism - outputs at constant rate regardless of input burstiness.

g.data-narration: Token Bucket allows bursty traffic by using tokens. Packets need tokens to transmit, and tokens regenerate at a constant rate.
g.text: More flexible than leaky bucket - allows burstiness while enforcing average rate.
β = bucket capacity, ρ = token rate

g.data-narration: TCP Slow Start begins with cwnd=1 MSS and doubles every RTT until packet loss, rapidly probing available bandwidth.
g.text: Initial phase: cwnd starts at 1 MSS and DOUBLES every RTT (exponential growth).

g.data-narration: In congestion avoidance, cwnd increases linearly by 1 MSS per RTT, cautiously probing for more available bandwidth.
g.text: After slow start: cwnd increases LINEARLY by 1 MSS per RTT - cautious probing.

g.data-narration: Fast Retransmit resends lost packets on duplicate ACKs without waiting for timeout, while Fast Recovery maintains throughput during recovery.
g.text: Quick recovery from packet loss using duplicate ACKs instead of waiting for timeout.

g.data-narration: TCP Tahoe uses slow start + fast retransmit, resetting to cwnd=1 on loss. TCP Reno adds fast recovery, halving cwnd for faster recovery.
g.text: Two major TCP variants with different congestion response strategies.
Reno recovers faster than Tahoe after packet loss

g.data-narration: ECN allows routers to signal congestion before packet loss by marking packets, enabling proactive rate reduction without drops.
g.text: Routers mark packets to signal congestion BEFORE dropping - proactive approach.

g.data-narration: Let's solve: Token rate=2/s, bucket=10 tokens, burst of 8 packets. Since bucket has 10 tokens, all 8 packets transmit immediately.
Given cwnd=1, ssthresh=16. RTT1: cwnd=2, RTT2: 4, RTT3: 8, RTT4: 16. At RTT5, we enter congestion avoidance.
g.text: RTT1: cwnd=2 | RTT2: cwnd=4 | RTT3: cwnd=8 | RTT4: cwnd=16
At RTT4: cwnd = ssthresh, so → Congestion Avoidance begins
Transition point: cwnd doubles until reaching ssthresh

g.data-narration: Remember these key formulas: Token bucket max burst, TCP slow start exponential growth, congestion avoidance linear growth, and multiplicative decrease.

To summarize: Congestion control prevents network overload. Flow control is receiver-focused, congestion control is network-focused. TCP uses slow start, avoidance, and recovery mechanisms. ECN enables lossless congestion handling.
g.text: • Congestion = demand > capacity → delays, loss, reduced throughput
